title: Refraction: how Fermat made light take the lazy way home
intro: Dip a long stick into still water and the stick will appear to break at the surface, continuing on at a different angle below. Children notice this; Greek philosophers wrote about it; Ptolemy in the second century filled tables with careful angle measurements and could find no rule. The clean rule came fifteen centuries later, and the deeper reason a generation after that, when Pierre de Fermat in 1662 pried open the geometry with a single strange principle: light takes the path that gets it there in the least time. From that one sentence falls all of Snell's law, and behind Snell's law a tradition that would eventually become the principle of least action and, with Feynman, the path integral.

sections.heading: 1. The stick that bends in water

blocks.text: The basic phenomenon is so old it has no discoverer. Anyone who has reached for a fish from above the waterline has felt the consequence: the fish is not where it appears. Light coming from the fish bends as it crosses from water into air, and the eye, knowing nothing of bending, simply traces the rays back along straight lines and locates the fish along the wrong one. The visual world quietly assumes a uniform medium, and water exposes the lie.

Hero of Alexandria, around 50 CE, had already proved one half of the puzzle. For a flat mirror he showed that the path light actually takes (the angle of incidence equal to the angle of reflection) is the shortest path between source and observer that touches the mirror. This was suggestive: light might be lazy. But reflection is the easy case, where the medium is the same on both sides and shortest path is also fastest path. Refraction, where light crosses between media, refused to give up its rule.

Ptolemy, around 150 CE, did the obvious experiment. He measured incidence and refraction angles between air, water, and glass, and tabulated them. He saw that for small angles the ratio θ₁/θ₂ was roughly constant, and for large angles it drifted, and he never figured out why. The error was in writing down the wrong quantity. The constancy is in the sines of the angles, not the angles themselves; for the small angles Ptolemy could measure cleanly the two are nearly identical, and the truth slipped past him. Sometimes a discovery waits not for a better experiment but for a better thing to measure.

The right thing to measure was first written down in Baghdad. In 984, Ibn Sahl, working in the House of Wisdom on a treatise about burning lenses, drew a diagram of a ray crossing a glass interface and labeled the geometry with a constant ratio that, in modern notation, is exactly the ratio of sines. His manuscript was rediscovered only in 1990, by which time European textbooks had been calling the rule Snell's law for three hundred years. Willebrord Snellius arrived at it in Leiden in 1621, never published, and was scooped by René Descartes, who put it in print in La Dioptrique in 1637 with no credit to Snell at all. Names stick by accident.

The law itself is one line. If light crosses from a medium where it travels at speed v₁ into one where it travels at v₂, and we define the index of refraction by n = c/v (so vacuum has n = 1, water n ≈ 1.33, ordinary glass n ≈ 1.5, diamond n ≈ 2.4), then the angles measured from the normal to the surface obey:

blocks.caption: Snell's law. n₁ is the refractive index of the first medium, n₂ of the second; θ₁ is the angle of the incoming ray measured from the normal to the surface, θ₂ the angle of the refracted ray on the other side. Refractive index n = c/v, the ratio of light's speed in vacuum to its speed in the medium. Read aloud: the product n sin θ is the same on both sides of the interface.

blocks.text: The product n sin θ is conserved across the interface. Light going from a low-n medium into a higher-n medium bends toward the normal (θ₂ < θ₁); going the other way, it bends away. Push the angle far enough on the way out and sin θ₂ would have to exceed 1, which it cannot, and the light is reflected entirely back. That is the cleanest geometric fact to come out of Snell's law: total internal reflection, the principle that traps light inside fiber optic cables and keeps the modern internet running.

But the law as stated is empirical. It tells you what happens; it does not tell you why the product n sin θ should be the conserved quantity, or why the same constant should govern interfaces between any two media. For that you need a deeper principle, and Descartes did not have one. He gave a cumbersome derivation involving tennis balls passing through cloth, which was wrong about the direction of the speed change (he had light going faster in glass, not slower) but happened to give the right ratio. Twenty-five years would pass before Fermat saw what Descartes had missed.

quiz.question: A ray of light strikes a glass surface (n = 1.5) from air (n = 1.0) at an angle of 30° from the normal. What is the angle of refraction inside the glass, and which way has the ray bent?
options: About 30°, the ray continues straight (Snell's law does not apply at this angle)
About 48.6°, bent away from the normal
About 19.5°, bent away from the normal
quiz.explanation: Apply n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂ with n₁ = 1.0, θ₁ = 30°, n₂ = 1.5. Then sin θ₂ = sin 30° / 1.5 = 0.5 / 1.5 = 0.333, giving θ₂ ≈ 19.5°. Light entering a denser medium (higher n) bends toward the normal because it slows down; the wave fronts on the slow side are closer together, and the geometry forces a smaller angle. The third option is the reverse case (going from glass to air); the fourth gets the magnitude right but the direction wrong.

sections.heading: 3. Fermat's wager: the path of least time

blocks.text: Fermat, a lawyer in Toulouse who did mathematics in his spare time and kept changing the face of it, distrusted Descartes's tennis-ball derivation. He proposed instead a single principle, audacious in its simplicity: of all the paths a ray of light could take from one point to another, it takes the one that minimizes the total travel time. In a uniform medium that is just a straight line, recovering geometric optics. At an interface where the speed changes, the minimum-time path is no longer the straight line; the light bends, and the bending angle is exactly Snell's.

The intuition is the lifeguard. You stand on a beach and see a swimmer in distress out in the water. You can run on sand at ten meters per second and swim at two. The shortest path (a straight line) wastes too much time in the slow medium; the fastest path bends, putting more length on the sand and less in the water. The optimal crossing point is the one where, if you tried to shift it left or right by an inch, the time you saved on one side would exactly equal the time you lost on the other. That balance condition, written out in geometry, is Snell's law.

Setting up the algebra: let the source be at height h₁ above the interface and the target at depth h₂ below, separated horizontally by distance D, and let x be the horizontal distance from the source to the crossing point. The total travel time is

blocks.caption: T(x) is the total travel time as a function of where the ray crosses the interface. h₁ is the height of the source above the interface, h₂ the depth of the target below; v₁ and v₂ are the wave speeds on the two sides; D is the horizontal distance between source and target; x is the horizontal distance from the source to the crossing point. Read aloud: total time equals path length over speed, summed for each leg, with x as the only thing we get to choose.

A source A sits in a fast medium (n₁ = 1.00) above the interface; a target B sits in a slow medium (n₂, adjustable) below. Drag the crossing point P along the interface and watch the total travel time T(x) plotted at the bottom. The straight-line path (P directly between A and B) is rarely the fastest; the minimum-time path bends, and the marked minimum is exactly the point where n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂. Adjust n₂ to see denser media bend the path harder.

blocks.text: Differentiate T with respect to x, set the derivative to zero, and the result simplifies to sin θ₁ / v₁ = sin θ₂ / v₂. Multiplying through by c gives n₁ sin θ₁ = n₂ sin θ₂. Snell's law is no longer an empirical law about angles; it is the geometric signature of a path that minimizes time. Notice what we did not assume: nothing about waves, nothing about particles, nothing about what light is. We assumed only that whatever light is, it minimizes its travel time. From that one premise, all of geometric optics falls out.

sections.heading: 4. Faster or slower in glass: the bet that took 200 years

blocks.text: Fermat's derivation and Descartes's derivation gave the same final equation, but they disagreed about a measurable physical fact. For Fermat, light bending toward the normal in glass meant light was slower in glass: the ray spent less of its length in the slow medium, exactly as the lifeguard runs more on sand than in water. For Descartes (and later Newton, who built on him), light bending toward the normal meant light was faster in glass: the medium attracted the corpuscles perpendicular to the surface, speeding them up in the same way a ball rolling off a table accelerates downward at the edge.

Newton's particle theory and Huygens's wave theory thus made opposite predictions for v_glass / v_air. Newton said greater than one; Huygens, treating light as a disturbance whose wavefronts crowd together when they slow down, said less than one. Settling the argument required measuring the speed of light inside a piece of glass or water, an experiment which in the seventeenth century was simply not possible. Roemer had not yet measured the speed of light in air, let alone in a denser medium, and the question stayed open for almost two centuries.

The decisive measurement came in 1850. Léon Foucault, in Paris, set up a rotating-mirror apparatus and ran two parallel beams of light, one through air and one through a tube of water, and bounced them off a distant mirror back through the rotating one. The rotating mirror had moved a tiny amount during the round trip, and the angular displacement of the returning beam was a direct measure of how long the light had taken. The water beam came back having lagged the air beam by exactly the factor n_water = 1.33. Light is slower in dense media. Huygens won; Newton lost; and Fermat, who had asserted the answer two centuries earlier on the basis of a principle, was vindicated.

There is a moral in this for the working physicist. Two derivations can give the same equation and disagree about the underlying mechanism, and the right way to pick between them is not to argue about which derivation is more elegant but to find an experiment whose outcome differs. Fermat had no way to make that measurement; he made the right call anyway, on aesthetic grounds. The aesthetic grounds turned out to be the universe's.

quiz.question: A scientist in 1830 must choose between Newton's and Huygens's theories of refraction. Both predict the same angles via Snell's law, but they disagree on a measurable quantity. Which experiment would settle the question, and what would each theory predict?
options: Measure the angle of total internal reflection in a glass prism; Newton predicts a smaller critical angle than Huygens
Measure the speed of light inside water relative to in air; Newton predicts faster in water, Huygens predicts slower
Measure the index of refraction of glass at different wavelengths; Newton predicts no dependence, Huygens predicts strong dependence
Measure the brightness of the refracted beam relative to the reflected one; Newton predicts more is reflected, Huygens predicts more is refracted
quiz.explanation: The crucial split is the speed inside a dense medium. Newton's particles are attracted toward the dense medium and speed up perpendicular to the surface; Huygens's wave fronts crowd together as the wave slows down. Both reproduce the angle observed at the surface, but they disagree on what light is doing on the other side of it. Foucault's 1850 rotating-mirror measurement showed light lags in water by the refractive-index factor, vindicating Huygens. The other options are tempting but wrong: total internal reflection follows from Snell's law alone (both theories agree), wavelength dependence (dispersion) was known in both camps, and brightness ratios involve electromagnetic boundary conditions neither theory had access to.

sections.heading: 5. The principle that would not stay in optics

blocks.text: Fermat's principle has the unusual property of not feeling like a physics law at all. It says light somehow knows, before it sets out, which path will get it there fastest. That this kind of statement could be physics scandalized many of his contemporaries, and the unease never quite went away. Newton's laws are local: a ball rolls forward because of the forces on it right now. Fermat's principle is global: the path is selected as a whole, by comparison to alternatives. The two pictures are mathematically equivalent, as Euler later showed, but they read like different worlds.

The deeper power of Fermat's idea showed up when other physicists started generalizing it. Maupertuis in 1744 proposed that mechanical systems also follow a principle of least action, where action is a particular integral over the path. Euler and Lagrange tightened the formulation, and by 1788 mechanics could be done either with Newton's forces or with a single variational principle that asked: of all the conceivable paths a particle could take between two events, which one has stationary action? Hamilton and Jacobi extended it further. By 1900 every fundamental theory of physics, from mechanics to electrodynamics, could be derived from a least-action principle, and the question had become not whether such a principle existed but what its action functional was.

Quantum mechanics gave the cleanest answer to the original puzzle. In Feynman's path-integral formulation, a particle does not, in fact, take the path of least time or least action. It takes every possible path simultaneously, with each path contributing a complex amplitude e^(iS/ℏ) where S is the action along that path. For most paths the phases cancel out by interference. Only near the path of stationary action do neighboring paths share roughly the same phase and add constructively, and what we see classically is the line where the contributions did not cancel. The bent stick in the bucket of water carries inside it the entire variational tradition: optics with Fermat, mechanics with Lagrange, the quantum world with Feynman, the same principle each time, larger every century.

finalQuiz.question: Fermat's principle states that light travels along the path of least time between two points. A modern student asks: how can light 'know' in advance which path is fastest? Which of the following best captures the resolution offered by quantum mechanics?
options: Light does not know in advance; the principle is only an approximation that breaks down at quantum scales, replaced by a strict local force law
Light explores all possible paths simultaneously as quantum amplitudes, and the classical path is where neighboring paths interfere constructively rather than canceling
Light has hidden internal degrees of freedom that compute the optimal path before emission, similar to a navigation system
Each photon is sent on a randomly selected path, and the appearance of a unique optimal path is a statistical illusion produced by averaging many photons
finalQuiz.explanation: Feynman's path integral resolves the puzzle by removing the apparent foreknowledge. A photon does not pick a single fastest path; it contributes amplitudes along every conceivable path, weighted by e^(iS/ℏ). Almost everywhere, the rapid phase variation between neighboring paths produces destructive interference and the contributions cancel. Only near the path of stationary action (which in optics is the path of least time) do nearby paths share a similar phase and add constructively, leaving the classical ray we observe. The first option misreads what quantum mechanics did to Fermat: it generalized the principle, it did not replace it. The third smuggles in classical intentionality. The fourth predicts a noisy diffuse beam, contrary to the sharp ray actually seen.
